I've also heard of people complaining about the summaries as well. How does that figure in to how investors look at this industry, which is facing massive blowback? MADISON MILLS, SENIOR A.I. REPORTER, "AXIOS": Yes, this A.I. hate wave that we're seeing could be a real problem for these A.I.
paragraphs.cn: Di CNN, saya ingin mendedahkan bahawa kami sedang menyaman Perplexity, menuduh syarikat itu menyalin dan berkongsi kandungan CNN secara haram dalam ringkasannya. Saya juga mendengar aduan tentang ringkasan ini. Bagaimana ini mempengaruhi pelabur melihat industri yang menghadapi tentangan hebat ini? Wartawan AI kanan Axios, Madison Mills: Ya, gelombang kebencian AI yang kita lihat ini boleh menjadi masalah sebenar bagi syarikat AI ini.

paragraphs.speaker: Audie Cornish
paragraphs.text: companies because they need something called compute that's like the currency, the energy fueling the A.I. race. If the A.I. labs can't get their data centers, then they can't get the compute that they need to service your queries.
paragraphs.cn: Kerana mereka memerlukan sesuatu yang dipanggil kuasa pengiraan, yang seperti mata wang, tenaga yang memacu perlumbaan AI. Jika makmal AI tidak dapat memperoleh pusat data, maka mereka tidak dapat memperoleh kuasa pengiraan yang diperlukan untuk memproses pertanyaan pengguna.

paragraphs.speaker: Mills
paragraphs.text: So, it could be a big problem. And we're seeing this data center pushback really hitting a fever pitch.
paragraphs.cn: Jadi, ini boleh menjadi masalah besar. Kita melihat tentangan terhadap pusat data ini benar-benar memuncak.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Yes, because some of them are going -- are supposed to be coming up with their own way to power -- to sort of self-power the data centers.
paragraphs.cn: Ya, kerana sebahagian daripada pusat data ini akan -- sepatutnya menghasilkan kuasa sendiri -- sedikit sebanyak berdikari.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: But, like, people don't want them. And I was thinking, when I look at how people talk about A.I., for instance, we played the president earlier and he said, "we're talking about it where the American people can benefit from the success of A.I. They're going to like it better because we're leading China, we're leading everyone in the world. We want to keep it that way." And I felt like, if you took away the word A.I. and put "globalization," we've heard this before.
paragraphs.cn: Tetapi, orang tidak mahu mereka. Saya terfikir, apabila saya melihat bagaimana orang bercakap tentang AI, seperti kita memainkan ucapan presiden tadi, dia berkata: "Kita sedang membincangkan bagaimana untuk memastikan rakyat Amerika mendapat manfaat daripada kejayaan AI. Mereka akan lebih menyukainya kerana kita mendahului China, mendahului semua negara di dunia. Kita mahu kekal seperti ini." Saya rasa, jika kita gantikan perkataan "AI" dengan "globalisasi", kita pernah mendengar ini sebelum ini.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: And I wonder if that is contributing to what you call the hate wave.
paragraphs.cn: Saya tertanya-tanya sama ada ini menyebabkan apa yang anda panggil "gelombang kebencian".

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: People feeling like, I'm being told something's going to make my life better. And my experience in the past is that that isn't -- doesn't play out the way politicians say it will.
paragraphs.cn: Orang rasa, seseorang memberitahu saya sesuatu akan menjadikan hidup saya lebih baik, tetapi pengalaman lalu saya ialah ia tidak akan menjadi kenyataan seperti yang dijanjikan oleh ahli politik.

paragraphs.speaker: Mills
paragraphs.text: I mean, I would push back on that too because the overwhelming research shows that people are not happy about A.I. There's this YouGov poll people cite all the time that showed very clear bipartisan support against A.I. across the aisle.
paragraphs.cn: Maksud saya, saya juga akan menentang itu, kerana majoriti kajian menunjukkan orang tidak berpuas hati dengan AI. Tinjauan YouGov yang sering dirujuk menunjukkan tentangan dwipartisan terhadap AI sangat jelas, merentasi garis parti.

paragraphs.speaker: Mills
paragraphs.text: And we're seeing that play out with the data center pushback that you had mentioned. I also do think part of the challenge is that these models are getting better so much faster than anything we've seen.
paragraphs.cn: Kita lihat, tentangan terhadap pusat data yang anda sebutkan sedang berlaku. Saya juga berpendapat sebahagian daripada cabarannya ialah model ini bertambah baik dengan lebih pantas daripada apa-apa yang pernah kita lihat sebelum ini.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Yes. Anthropic releasing a safer work mythos.
paragraphs.cn: Ya. Anthropic mengeluarkan mitos kerja yang lebih selamat.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Which is still scary.
paragraphs.cn: Ini masih menakutkan.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: After saying that they were so worried about it they only brought it to the government and certain companies.
paragraphs.cn: Selepas menyatakan mereka sangat bimbang tentang perkara ini, mereka hanya menyediakannya kepada kerajaan dan syarikat tertentu.

paragraphs.speaker: Mills
paragraphs.text: Exactly. And because of that, I think that people haven't necessarily seen a lot of the benefits of A.I. because it's getting better so much faster. And so people are, you know, hearing about a data center in their backyard, they're hearing A.I. CEOs say that it could replace their jobs. And, by the way, you haven't really felt the positive use cases of it yet because it's getting more technical more quickly. So, I think that creates kind of a challenging soup for people. Yes.
paragraphs.cn: Betul. Kerana itu, saya rasa orang tidak semestinya melihat banyak manfaat AI, kerana ia bertambah baik terlalu cepat. Jadi, orang dengar pusat data akan dibina di halaman belakang rumah mereka, dengar CEO AI kata ia mungkin menggantikan pekerjaan mereka. Dan, secara kebetulan, anda belum benar-benar merasai kesan positifnya, kerana teknologinya meningkat terlalu cepat. Jadi, saya rasa ini mewujudkan situasi yang sukar bagi orang.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Yes. We'll see what happens when people are ringing that bell on Wall Street. Madison Mills, thank you so much for being here. Always love to have you. And straight ahead on CNN THIS MORNING, the World Cup kicks off tomorrow. But with sky high ticket prices, problems with immigration, is this going to be the World Cup of chaos? Plus, Nancy Mace broke with President Trump over the Epstein files. Is that what costs her a shot at the governor's mansion in South Carolina, or was it something else?
paragraphs.cn: Ya. Kita tunggu dan lihat apa yang berlaku apabila loceng Wall Street dibunyikan. Madison Mills, terima kasih banyak kerana datang. Kami sentiasa gembira anda datang. Seterusnya dalam CNN This Morning, Piala Dunia bermula esok. Tetapi harga tiket tinggi, isu imigresen timbul, adakah Piala Dunia ini akan menjadi kucar-kacir? Selain itu, Nancy Mace berpecah dengan Presiden Trump berhubung dokumen Epstein. Adakah ini menghilangkan peluangnya untuk menjadi gabenor Carolina Selatan, atau adakah sebab lain?

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: So, the World Cup kicks off tomorrow. I know, I said it. It kicks off across North America. But are the off the field headlines threatening to overshadow the biggest tournament in the world? So, there was a Somali referee turned away by U.S. Customs and Border Patrol in Miami when he arrived for his World Cup assignment. And he received a hero's welcome when he returned to Somalia. The Trump administration tells CNN that vetting uncovered derogatory information. They did not go into further detail.
paragraphs.cn: Jadi, Piala Dunia bermula esok. Saya tahu, saya cakap. Ia akan berlangsung di seluruh Amerika Utara. Tetapi adakah berita di luar padang akan mengancam perhatian terhadap acara sukan terbesar di dunia? Seorang pengadil Somalia ditolak masuk oleh Kastam dan Perlindungan Sempadan AS di Miami, yang sepatutnya menjalankan tugas Piala Dunia. Dia disambut seperti wira apabila kembali ke Somalia. Pentadbiran Trump memberitahu CNN bahawa semakan mendapati maklumat yang tidak menguntungkan, tetapi tidak mendedahkan butiran lanjut.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Now, the ref told "The New York Times," quote, "I'm just simply a referee who's trying to live his dream." Fans from Scotland said their permits were withdrawn at the final moment. Teams from Senegal, Uzbekistan were treated, quote, "like criminals," according to critics. And a player from Iraq was questioned for seven hours upon arrival in Chicago.
paragraphs.cn: Pengadil itu memberitahu New York Times: "Saya hanya seorang pengadil yang cuba merealisasikan impian." Peminat dari Scotland berkata lesen mereka dibatalkan pada saat-saat akhir. Pengkritik berkata pasukan Senegal dan Uzbekistan dilayan "seperti penjenayah." Seorang pemain Iraq disoal siasat selama tujuh jam selepas tiba di Chicago.

paragraphs.speaker: Ian Wright
paragraphs.text: Every few hours it's another story. Another story about fans denied, players denied, officials denied, journalists denied. Now refs. You know something? I'm laughing, but it's not funny. It's actually not funny. Something has to be said. It's expensive tickets. Most expensive tickets ever. Know what? This is the World Cup. This is a World Cup of chaos.
paragraphs.cn: Setiap beberapa jam ada cerita baru. Satu lagi cerita tentang peminat ditolak, pemain ditolak, pegawai ditolak, wartawan ditolak. Sekarang giliran pengadil. Anda tahu? Saya ketawa, tetapi ini tidak lucu. Sebenarnya langsung tidak lucu. Sesuatu mesti diperkatakan. Tiket mahal, paling mahal dalam sejarah. Anda tahu? Ini Piala Dunia. Ini Piala Dunia yang kucar-kacir.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Joining us in the group chat, Dan Courtemanche. He's the executive vice president and chief communications officer for Major League Soccer. Did I say your name right? I don't want your mom being mad at me.
paragraphs.cn: Sertai kami dalam perbualan kumpulan ialah Dan Courtemanche, Naib Presiden Eksekutif dan Ketua Pegawai Komunikasi Major League Soccer. Saya sebut nama awak dengan betul? Saya tak nak mak awak marah saya.

paragraphs.speaker: Dan Courtemanche
paragraphs.text: You did. Thank you. It's terrific to be here. Really appreciate it.
paragraphs.cn: Awak betul. Terima kasih. Gembira berada di sini. Terima kasih banyak.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: So, I wanted to talk to you about this for a couple of reasons. One, your team sent over some information here, and I was noting that there is a record number of MLS players, 45, who represent countries in this, right?
paragraphs.cn: Jadi, saya nak bincang dengan awak tentang ini, atas beberapa sebab. Pertama, pasukan awak hantar beberapa maklumat, saya perhatikan ada rekod 45 pemain MLS mewakili negara masing-masing, kan?

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: And that you've also got -- done a lot of work through your academies to be a place to recruit people who will want to play here. Does this -- these headlines, do they kind of undermine that message that MLS has been so good, frankly, at cultivating over the last decade?
paragraphs.cn: Dan awak juga banyak bekerja melalui sistem pembangunan belia, menjadi tempat untuk merekrut mereka yang mahu bermain di sini. Adakah berita ini melemahkan imej yang dibina MLS dengan teliti dalam dekad yang lalu?

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.text: Yes, I think what we're going to see is some of those headlines tomorrow when the World Cup opens in Mexico City, then certainly in North America and Canada and the United States on Friday. It's going to shift from some of those headlines, the World Cup of chaos, to celebration, to pageantry, to a party.
paragraphs.cn: Ya, saya rasa esok apabila Piala Dunia dibuka di Mexico City, dan sudah tentu pada hari Jumaat di Amerika Utara, Kanada dan AS, kita akan lihat beberapa berita ini. Tumpuan akan beralih daripada berita "Piala Dunia kucar-kacir" kepada sambutan, kemeriahan dan pesta.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: But I'm saying, for the players, is this irritating, scary, frustrating? The whole point is international cooperation.
paragraphs.cn: But I want to say, for the players, is this annoying, scary, frustrating? The whole point is international cooperation.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: It's not feeling welcoming.
paragraphs.cn: It feels a bit unwelcoming.

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.text: So, I hear you. You know, ultimately, that's a question for the players. It's not something that we've seen. And in Major League Soccer, we have players from 78 different countries. And we've been around for 31 seasons. And we haven't experienced that, challenges with visa issues or anything. But I understand where you're coming from. We want to be welcoming to all the players. We want to see them have a wonderful time here in the United States, Canada and Mexico.
paragraphs.cn: So, I hear you. You know, at the end of the day, it's the players' issue. We haven't seen that. In Major League Soccer, we have players from 78 different countries. We've been operating for 31 seasons. We haven't faced challenges like visa issues. But I understand your point. We want to welcome all players. We want to see them have a great time in the US, Canada, and Mexico.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: And a lot of your incoming star players, over the last couple of years, coming to the U.S., they're coming from abroad. It's finally a place people are starting to come to and want to play, right?
paragraphs.cn: In the past few years, many star players coming to the US are from abroad. It has finally become a place where people start to aspire to come and play, right?

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.text: Most definitely. The greatest of all time. Lionel Messi, by the way, scored last night in Auburn, Alabama, in a friendly against Iceland. You'll see him on Tuesday in Kansas City against Algeria. I hope he scores a lot of goals because then you can watch him playing for Inter Miami each and every weekend in Major League Soccer.
paragraphs.cn: No doubt. The greatest player of all time. By the way, Lionel Messi scored last night in a warm-up match against Iceland in Auburn, Alabama. You'll see him on Tuesday in Kansas City against Algeria. I hope he scores many goals so you can watch him play for Inter Miami every weekend in MLS.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Now, what I loved about that person who spoke at the end of our introduction is he goes, oh, I laugh because, you know, it's sort of -- sort of sad. And then he tied all of that to ticket prices. And I thought that was really fascinating, that people are also looking at this in the context of the cost of the ticket. The average get in price for a World Cup match right now, roughly $600 for the opening round of the tournament. And people are complaining about FIFA's pricing scheme.
paragraphs.cn: Now, I liked the person who spoke at the end of our introduction, who said, oh, I laughed because, you know, it's a bit -- a bit sad. Then he linked it all to ticket prices. I find it interesting that people are looking at it from a ticket price perspective. The average entry price for World Cup matches right now, group stage, is about $600. People are complaining about FIFA's pricing scheme.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: And right now, if I want, I can still get a ticket to USA Paraguay. It's not sold out, and it should be.
paragraphs.cn: Now, if I wanted, I could still buy tickets for USA vs. Paraguay. The match isn't sold out yet, but it should be.

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.text: Yes, I can tell you, I'm going to be there for the U.S.- Paraguay game. I certainly anticipate it's going to be sold out. I got to tell you, there's unprecedented demand for the World Cup.
paragraphs.cn: Yes, I can tell you, I will go watch USA vs. Paraguay. I expect it will definitely sell out. I have to tell you, demand for the World Cup is unprecedented.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Then why isn't it sold out?
paragraphs.cn: Then why isn't it sold out yet?

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.text: Much like you'll see from my Knicks tonight, where the get-in price is about $15,000 for lower level seating. Now, that said, I think you're going to see the pageantry, the celebration throughout the World Cup. But I understand where people are coming from. Also, you have to remind people that FIFA is actually a non-profit. So, all that money goes back to the 211 FIFA nations to build the sport, especially in some underdeveloped countries.
paragraphs.cn: Just like you'll see my Knicks game tonight, lower-level seats are about $15,000. That said, I think you'll see the spectacle and celebration during the World Cup. But I understand people's thinking. Also, you have to remind people that FIFA is actually a non-profit organization. So, all the money goes back to the 211 FIFA member associations to develop football, especially in less developed countries.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: OK, I'm glad you brought that up because we did have a California lawmaker, Representative Sydney Kamlager-Dove. She was on the show Friday. She said something I didn't expect to hear related to ticket prices and what her concerns were. Here's what she had to say. REP. SYDNEY KAMLAGER-DOVE (D-CA): The issue is dynamic pricing. The other issue is, you buy a ticket, you don't know where that seat is going to be. You're spending over $1,000.
paragraphs.cn: Okay, glad you mentioned that, because we do have a California congresswoman, Rep. Sydney Kamlager-Dove. She was on the show Friday. She said something I didn't expect to hear about ticket prices and her concerns. Here's what she said. Rep. Sydney Kamlager-Dove (D-CA): The problem is dynamic pricing. Another issue is, you buy a ticket and don't know where the seat is. You spend over $1,000.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: And finally you got the -- you get the seat and you're behind a post and you can't see anything. We want to know why ticket prices are changing so dramatically from city to city. I want to know why cities aren't getting any financial support from the World Cup to actually put on the games, because World Cup is getting everything, concessions, merchandise, parking, tickets.
paragraphs.cn: Finally you get a seat, and you're behind a pillar, seeing nothing. We want to know why ticket prices vary so much between cities. I want to know why cities don't get any financial support from the World Cup to host matches, because the World Cup takes everything: concessions, merchandise, parking, tickets.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: That was just a long list. Can you answer any of it?
paragraphs.cn: Soalan ini memang banyak. Bolehkah anda menjawab mana-mana satu?

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.text: Well, I can tell you, look, we're fortunate, five of the stadiums out of the 13 markets in the United States and Canada are Major League Soccer stadiums. And I can tell you that nobody will be sitting behind posts during the World Cup games in those MLS stadiums. So, look, she brings up some valid points. She's clearly an expert on it. But we think it's going to pivot again from some of the challenges that we've seen in the media to the celebration.
paragraphs.cn: Ya, saya boleh beritahu anda, kita bernasib baik kerana 5 daripada 13 stadium di Amerika Syarikat dan Kanada adalah stadium MLS. Saya boleh beritahu anda, semasa perlawanan Piala Dunia, tiada siapa yang akan duduk di belakang tiang di stadium MLS itu. Dia mengemukakan beberapa pandangan yang munasabah, dia jelas pakar dalam bidang ini. Tetapi kami fikir, tumpuan akan beralih daripada beberapa cabaran yang dilihat media kepada sambutan.

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.text: And ultimately goals scored by hopefully the United States national team and they make a deep run in the tournament.
paragraphs.cn: Akhirnya, diharapkan pasukan kebangsaan AS akan menjaringkan gol dan mara lebih jauh dalam kejohanan.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Yes. I think, fundamentally, nobody is arguing that it won't be good. They'll be arguing about whether or not they got to participate.
paragraphs.cn: Ya. Saya rasa, pada dasarnya tiada siapa yang akan mempersoalkan bahawa ini adalah perkara yang baik. Apa yang mereka akan perdebatkan adalah sama ada mereka boleh mengambil bahagian.

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.text: Yes. You know, and I -- you know, it's also a good reminder that FIFA actually had $60 tickets available for those passionate supporters who registered early. So, they had some affordable prices. But I certainly understand you. And for us, you also have to look at, there'll be nearly 80 fan festivals throughout the different markets. Twenty of our MLS clubs are going to have soccer celebrations. So, you're providing free accessibility to fans who may not be able to go to the games.
paragraphs.cn: Ya. Anda tahu, ini juga mengingatkan kita bahawa FIFA sebenarnya menyediakan tiket $60 untuk peminat yang mendaftar awal. Jadi, mereka ada harga yang berpatutan. Tetapi saya faham awak. Bagi kami, anda juga akan melihat bahawa akan ada hampir 80 festival peminat di pasaran yang berbeza. 20 kelab MLS kami akan mengadakan sambutan bola sepak. Dengan cara itu, mereka yang mungkin tidak dapat pergi ke stadium boleh turut serta secara percuma.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: OK. Well, thank you, Dan, so much for being here. Good luck. I know this is your Super Bowl. That's what we say here in the U.S. instead of soccer.
paragraphs.cn: Baik. Terima kasih banyak kerana datang ke sini, Dan. Semoga berjaya. Saya tahu ini Super Bowl anda -- -- kami katakan begitu di AS, bukan bola sepak.

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.text: Hey, I'm a big -- I'm a University of Georgia graduate. I love gridiron football. Come on.
paragraphs.cn: Hei, saya peminat tegar -- -- saya graduan Universiti Georgia. Saya suka bola sepak Amerika. Sungguh.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Oh, OK. OK. All right. Well, thank you for being here. Appreciate your time.
paragraphs.cn: Oh, baik. Baik. Baiklah. Terima kasih kerana datang. Terima kasih atas masa anda.

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.text: Really appreciate it.
paragraphs.cn: Terima kasih banyak.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Next on CNN THIS MORNING, Congresswoman Nancy Mace gets crushed in the South Carolina governor's race. Did going against Trump cost her the election? But first.
paragraphs.cn: Seterusnya dalam CNN This Morning, Ahli Kongres Nancy Mace tewas teruk dalam perlumbaan gabenor Carolina Selatan. Adakah menentang Trump menyebabkan dia kalah? Tapi pertama.

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Male
paragraphs.text: If you're elected governor, would you continue to perform exorcisms while governor?
paragraphs.cn: Jika anda dipilih sebagai gabenor, adakah anda akan terus melakukan eksorsisme sepanjang penggal?

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Yes, controversial candidate leading in the polls in Colorado. Will the strange headlines sway voters?
paragraphs.cn: Ya, calon kontroversi Colorado mendahului dalam tinjauan. Adakah tajuk pelik ini mempengaruhi pengundi?

paragraphs.speaker: Kyle Clark
paragraphs.text: Your campaign website falsely claimed that you had rescued more than 45,000 women and children. You once called in a U.S. military airstrike that killed 70 ISIS fighters. You perform exorcisms, commanding demons to come out of people. Do you understand why so many of your critics think you're a con man? VICTOR MARX (R),
paragraphs.cn: Laman web kempen anda secara palsu mendakwa anda menyelamatkan lebih 45,000 wanita dan kanak-kanak. Anda memanggil serangan udara AS yang membunuh 70 militan ISIS. Anda mengusir syaitan dan memerintahkan roh jahat keluar dari orang. Adakah anda faham mengapa begitu ramai pengkritik menganggap anda penipu? Victor Marx (Republikan),

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: OK, that was a wild ride. And it didn't stop there. That reporter in Colorado was interviewing a Republican frontrunner for governor, Victor Marx. He's a Marine Corps veteran. He runs a faith- based nonprofit. But he's made a lot of outlandish claims. And it does not end with exorcisms and rescuing women and children from slavery. He also says he killed a man when he was a child.
paragraphs.cn: Baiklah, itu satu perbualan yang gila. Dan ia belum berakhir. Wartawan dari Colorado itu sedang menemu bual calon utama Republikan untuk gabenor, Victor Marx. Dia seorang veteran Marin. Dia mengendalikan sebuah pertubuhan bukan untung berasaskan iman. Tetapi dia telah membuat banyak kenyataan yang keterlaluan. Dan bukan hanya tentang mengusir syaitan dan menyelamatkan wanita dan kanak-kanak daripada perhambaan. Dia juga kata dia pernah membunuh orang semasa kecil.

paragraphs.speaker: Kyle Clark
paragraphs.text: Your claim that your abusive stepfather forced you to kill a man when you were seven years old, is that the only person you've ever killed? VICTOR MARX (R),
paragraphs.cn: Anda mendakwa bapa tiri anda memaksa anda membunuh seseorang ketika berusia tujuh tahun. Adakah itu satu-satunya orang yang anda bunuh? Victor Marx (Republikan),

paragraphs.speaker: Colorado Candidate For Governor
paragraphs.text: Well, I would say, as a -- as a child, yes. But I've been in other situations where, you know, possibly people or persons died.
paragraphs.cn: Saya rasa, sebagai seorang kanak-kanak, ya. Tetapi saya juga pernah melalui situasi lain di mana, anda tahu, mungkin ada orang yang mati.

paragraphs.speaker: Clark
paragraphs.text: Do you think that you've killed people as an adult?
paragraphs.cn: Adakah anda fikir anda pernah membunuh seseorang semasa dewasa?

paragraphs.speaker: Clark
paragraphs.text: Killing somebody is a pretty weighty thing. So, I'm just asking you, how many people have you killed?
paragraphs.cn: Membunuh adalah perkara yang agak berat. Jadi, saya hanya bertanya, berapa ramai orang yang anda bunuh?

paragraphs.speaker: Marx
paragraphs.text: Well, if I did, I wouldn't be telling a reporter sitting here in my training center.
paragraphs.cn: Jika saya bunuh, saya tidak akan memberitahu wartawan yang duduk di pusat latihan saya.

paragraphs.speaker: Clark
paragraphs.text: How about voters? Would you tell voters?
paragraphs.cn: Bagaimana dengan pengundi? Adakah anda akan memberitahu pengundi?

paragraphs.speaker: Marx
paragraphs.text: Theres no need. I don't think that's important.
paragraphs.cn: Tidak perlu. Saya rasa itu tidak penting.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: OK. Group chat is back. I wanted to talk about this because we started the hour talking about Maine, right, and Graham Platner and being like, voters, what are they thinking? You know, like, what's happening? What will these scandals matter? And then I was seeing a lot more stories about what's happening in Colorado. Just to give you a sense of where things are. In this Republican primary right now, the fundraising, Victor Marx is at $2.7 million.
paragraphs.cn: Baik. Kumpulan sembang kembali. Saya mahu bincangkan ini kerana kita mulakan jam ini dengan bercakap tentang Maine, kan, dan Graham Platter, apa yang difikirkan oleh pengundi? Anda tahu, apa yang berlaku? Apakah kesan skandal ini? Kemudian saya nampak lebih banyak laporan tentang apa yang berlaku di Colorado. Untuk memberi anda gambaran. Dalam pemilihan utama Republikan ini, dari segi pengumpulan dana, Victor Marx ada $2.7 juta.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: The next closest person is at around $500,000, who is a state senator. I know you're just kind of clocking into this, Jarrett, but help me understand why he's so far ahead in the money. That means people are the donors. The people with pockets are giving to him. JARRETT STEPMAN, CONTRIBUTOR, "THE DAILY SIGNAL": Yes. In fairness, that may shift a bit after this moment, seeing him not as credible candidate.
paragraphs.cn: Tempat kedua ada kira-kira $500,000, seorang senator negeri. Saya tahu anda baru sahaja menyertai perbincangan, Jarrett, tetapi bantu saya faham mengapa dia mendahului dari segi dana. Ini bermakna orang ramai adalah penderma. Orang kaya memberi dia wang. Penulis The Daily Signal, Jarrett Stepman: Ya. Secara adilnya, selepas melihat dia bukan calon yang boleh dipercayai, keadaan mungkin berubah.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Well, that's a shift by about a million bucks. Yes.
paragraphs.cn: Itu kira-kira perbezaan satu juta dolar. Ya.

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.text: It would -- it would have to be quite a bit.
paragraphs.cn: That would require a lot of changes.

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.text: And, frankly, it -- Colorado, look, they haven't elected a Republican governor in that state since --
paragraphs.cn: And, frankly, Colorado, you see, they haven't elected a Republican governor since...

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.text: So, it's been a long time. I think you sometimes see this in states that have gone one way for a long time. You get sometimes candidates who are not particularly credible. There's a sort of a desperation saying, this guy seems like an outsider. He's, said, look, we saw this in Maine, somebody who's an outsider, in that case actually had a huge amount of success against a political establishment.
paragraphs.cn: So, it's been a long time. I think you sometimes see this in states that have leaned one way for a long time. Sometimes there are candidates who are less credible. There's a sense of desperation that says, this guy looks like an outsider. He says, look, we saw it in Maine, an outsider, actually in that case, had great success against the political establishment.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Can I tell you how far outside --
paragraphs.cn: Can I tell you how much of an outsider he is?

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Just how far outside. MIKE LEON, HOST, "CAN WE PLEASE TALK"
paragraphs.cn: How much of an outsider. "Can We Talk" host Mike Leon:

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.text: A bit outside is probably -- (CROSS TALK)
paragraphs.cn: A little more outsider might be -- -- (cross talk)

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: I'm going to -- I needed to be, like, what's happening with this exorcism bit? Is this an internet joke? Is this people sort of, you know, I don't know what I thought. But here's what I saw. VICTOR MARX (R),
paragraphs.cn: I'm going to -- -- I need to know, what's this exorcism bit? Is it an internet joke? Or are people a little, you know, I don't know what to think. But this is what I saw. Victor Marks (Republican),

paragraphs.speaker: Colorado Candidate For Governor
paragraphs.text: When you say I perform exorcisms.
paragraphs.cn: When you say I perform exorcisms.

paragraphs.speaker: Kyle Clark
paragraphs.text: Yes. You described yourself as a, quote/unquote, reluctant exorcist.
paragraphs.cn: Yes. You describe yourself as a, quote, reluctant exorcist.

paragraphs.speaker: Clark
paragraphs.text: Yes. Describe to me how your exorcisms work when you command demons out.
paragraphs.cn: Yes. Describe how your exorcism works, when you command the demon to leave.

paragraphs.speaker: Marx
paragraphs.text: Well, that's a joke. We identify the demon, like Jesus did. We asked what their name is. And then we give them commands. And they very frequently and typically will answer through the person's mind. They'll tell us what they hear. And then we destroy them, banish them to perfect judgment from Christ.
paragraphs.cn: Well, it's a joke. We identify demons, like Jesus did. We ask their names. Then we give them commands. They often and usually answer through the person's mind. They tell us what they heard. Then we destroy them, banishing them to the perfect judgment of Christ.

paragraphs.speaker: Clark
paragraphs.text: If you're elected governor, would you continue to perform exorcisms while governor?
paragraphs.cn: If you are elected governor, will you continue to perform exorcisms while in office?

paragraphs.speaker: Marx
paragraphs.text: I would never stop praying for people.
paragraphs.cn: I will never stop praying for people.

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.text: So, Audie, remember when we were talking about candidate quality earlier in the first segment. Shout out to Kyle Clark, the reporter there, for 9News in Denver. I love watching you on social media. He's done a lot on this one.
paragraphs.cn: So, Audi, remember earlier in the first paragraph we discussed candidate qualities? Shout out to Kyle Clark, a reporter from Denver 9News. I love watching you on social media. He's done a lot on this.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: He's actually become a bit of a social media star precisely for this coverage.
paragraphs.cn: He actually became a social media star because of this report.

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.text: He has. Precisely for this and the pushback. He's asked different questions of different candidates to give them factual examples. And the fact that he did this, I think it truly exposes a candidate. The fact that he has 2.7, like you said, million dollars in campaign donations. It should be alarming for all of us given this guy's track record and his history and what he just said in that interview there. We're all trying to make sense of exorcisms. And this morning about a candidate, I mean, come on. (CROSS TALK)
paragraphs.cn: He really did. Because of this and the backlash. He asked different candidates different questions, giving them factual examples. By doing so, I think he really exposed the candidates. As you said, he has $2.7 million in campaign donations. Considering this person's track record, history, and what he just said in the interview, it should shock all of us. We're all trying to understand exorcism. And this morning about a candidate, I mean, come on. (Cross talk)

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Yes. But, you know what, I think I also thought, like, OK, Marines, if he's a guy talking about the war, maybe people are interested in that. If he's a guy talking about Epstein, which he is, somebody who is constantly talking about child trafficking, there are lanes within the MAGA movement that are laser focused on this topic.
paragraphs.cn: Yes. But, you know, I also thought, okay, Marine, if he's talking about war, maybe people are interested in that. If he's talking about Epstein, like he does, a person constantly talking about child trafficking, then in the MAGA movement, there are some factions very focused on this topic.

paragraphs.speaker: Max Rose
paragraphs.text: Yes. Yes, I'll say this as a partizan Democrat to my Republican friends and colleagues, that's your guy. That's your nominee. They should vote for him. They should rally for him.
paragraphs.cn: Yes. Yes, as a Democrat, I want to say to my Republican friends and colleagues, that's your guy. That's your candidate. They should vote for him. They should rally for him.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: I think this is sarcasm. I think under his name it should say Max Rose being sarcastic.
paragraphs.cn: I think this is sarcasm. I think under his name it should say Max Rose in sarcasm.

paragraphs.speaker: Rose
paragraphs.text: They should -- we should (INAUDIBLE). This is their pathway to finally take back Colorado. (CROSS TALK)
paragraphs.cn: They should -- -- we should (inaudible). This is their path to finally take back Colorado. (Cross talk)

paragraphs.speaker: Rose
paragraphs.text: Us Democrats are so afraid of this guy. And, you know,
paragraphs.cn: We Democrats are very afraid of this person. And, you know,

paragraphs.speaker: I -- Leon
paragraphs.text: Republicans just learned about him.
paragraphs.cn: Republicans just got to know him.

paragraphs.speaker: Rose
paragraphs.text: Oh, my God. We're --
paragraphs.cn: Oh my god. We -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Listen. No, OK, what you're saying, I can see you amused, but, however, here's Lauren Boebert of Colorado giving her word on why she thinks this is a decent path. REP. LAUREN BOEBERT (R-CO): So, I think right now in Colorado we need a motivator who can then lead. Motivation has to be key in any of our Republican races, especially in a non-election year. I believe that Victor Marx will get qualified people in positions. I stand ready to help him. I am not disparaging any of the other candidates.
paragraphs.cn: Listen. No, okay, what are you saying, I see you think it's funny, but, however, this is Lauren Boebert from Colorado explaining why she thinks this is a decent path. Rep. Lauren Boebert (R-CO): So, I think now in Colorado, we need a motivator who can lead. Motivation must be key in any Republican campaign, especially in a non-election year. I believe Victor Marx will put qualified people in positions. I am ready to help him. I am not disparaging any other candidates.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: I have endorsed Victor Marx, but that's not something that I've turned the volume way up on. I want candidates to be able to professionally run their own race. I do not like the hateful back and forth that I see with Republicans.
paragraphs.cn: Saya menyokong Victor Marx, tetapi ini bukan sesuatu yang saya hebohkan. Saya mahu calon menjalankan kempen secara profesional. Saya tidak suka melihat pertengkaran penuh kebencian antara Republikan.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Today is a day of ringing endorsements, but --
paragraphs.cn: Hari ini penuh dengan sokongan padu, tetapi -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.text: I said it, don't (ph) turn up the volume.
paragraphs.cn: Saya kata, jangan (tidak jelas) besarkan suara.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: No, no, this is the -- we talked about this during the break. The imperfect messenger argument. We heard this with Trump, right?
paragraphs.cn: Tidak, tidak, ini -- -- apa yang kita bincangkan semasa rehat. Hujah utusan yang tidak sempurna. Kita juga dengar tentang Trump, kan?

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: You're an evangelical. Maybe you're like, I don't know about the marriages, I don't know about this, I don't know about that. But maybe he will put in people that will do the thing we want.
paragraphs.cn: Awak seorang evangelis. Mungkin awak fikir, saya tidak tahu tentang perkahwinannya, saya tidak tahu ini, saya tidak tahu itu. Tapi mungkin dia akan lantik orang yang boleh buat apa yang kita mahu.

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.text: It's the outsider again. Look, I mean, in Maine, you had a guy who has a Nazi tattoo fighting the fascists. Was that a credible campaign? Maybe it wasn't.
paragraphs.cn: Ini lagi orang luar. Lihat, maksud saya, di Maine, ada seorang dengan tatu Nazi yang lawan fasis. Itu kempen yang boleh dipercayai? Mungkin tidak.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Maybe he needed an exorcist.
paragraphs.cn: Mungkin dia perlukan pengusir hantu.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: And maybe he should call Colorado.
paragraphs.cn: Mungkin dia patut hubungi Colorado.

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.text: But again, that's also an outsider candidate that people see as different from the establishment as it is in the state. If they think things are going the wrong way, even a charismatic, let's call it, candidate like this can punch through. Yes, the other side may say, hey, we want that guy. We want to face him. But sometimes you get what you wish for and it turns up backfiring.
paragraphs.cn: Tapi sekali lagi, ini calon luar, orang fikir dia berbeza dengan penubuh negeri. Jika mereka rasa keadaan menuju ke arah salah, walaupun calon yang karismatik, katakanlah, macam ini boleh menonjol. Ya, pihak lain mungkin kata, hey, kami nak orang tu. Kami nak lawan dia. Tapi kadang-kadang awak dapat apa yang awak nak, tapi jadi sebaliknya.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Yes, that does backfire. I have one more thing about being an outsider. This one is about a member of Congress who had hoped to go into a new office in the next year. They're coming up short. Last night Republican Nancy Mace finished fifth in the GOP primary for South Carolina governor. She did not get the president's endorsement in the race. She believes it was because of her vote to release the Epstein files, which may have eventually led to her defeat. REP.
paragraphs.cn: Ya, itu memang jadi sebaliknya. Saya ada satu lagi perkara tentang orang luar. Ini melibatkan seorang ahli kongres yang berharap masuk pejabat baru tahun depan. Mereka sedang ketinggalan. Semalam, Republikan Nancy Mace berada di tempat kelima dalam pemilihan utama gabenor Republikan South Carolina. Dia tidak mendapat sokongan presiden dalam kempen. Dia fikir ini kerana dia mengundi untuk mendedahkan fail Epstein, yang mungkin akhirnya menyebabkan kekalahannya.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: NANCY MACE (R-SC): I chose to expose the abusers of children, and apparently I chose wrong if the goal was winning an election. I'm at peace with that because when a candidate is OK with corruption, when a candidate is OK with cover ups, something is broken. And that's not political opinion, that is a moral emergency.
paragraphs.cn: Nancy Mace (R-SC): Saya pilih untuk mendedahkan mereka yang dera kanak-kanak, jelas jika matlamatnya menang pilihan raya, saya pilih salah. Saya tenang dengan itu, kerana apabila calon bertolak ansur dengan rasuah, apabila calon bertolak ansur dengan penutupan, ada sesuatu yang rosak. Itu bukan pandangan politik, itu krisis moral.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Group chat is back. I'm going to just say it. There's been a lot of difficult headlines for Nancy Mace. Another person who is dealing with scandal and allegations. But at the same time, she is another person who talked about Epstein a lot, right? She's another person who got on the wrong side of Trump on certain issues. Is that reflected here, or is this really candidate quality?
paragraphs.cn: Sembang kumpulan kembali. Saya cakap terus. Nancy Mace ada banyak tajuk rumit. Seorang lagi yang hadapi skandal dan tuduhan. Tapi pada masa sama, dia juga seorang yang selalu cakap tentang Epstein, kan? Dia bertentangan dengan Trump dalam beberapa isu. Ini tercermin di sini, atau ini isu kualiti calon?

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.text: I want to go back to something we talked about in Maine with Susan Collins, talking about the issues, and Platner as well, talking about issues to Mainers. I feel like South Carolinians didn't hear Nancy Mace talking about that. And more of those headlines and more of her getting a microphone in front of her --
paragraphs.cn: Saya ingin kembali ke topik yang kita bincangkan dengan Susan Collins di Maine, bercakap tentang isu, dan Plattner, bercakap tentang isu kepada rakyat Maine. Saya rasa rakyat South Carolina tidak mendengar Nancy Mace bercakap tentang perkara ini. Lebih kepada tajuk utama, dan dia memegang mikrofon -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.text: Or coming on this network and arguing with somebody and then saying something completely the opposite when she gets off camera, I think those things are what really caught up to her in this race.
paragraphs.cn: Atau datang ke rangkaian ini untuk bertengkar dengan seseorang, kemudian berkata perkara yang bertentangan sepenuhnya selepas turun pentas, saya fikir itulah sebab dia benar-benar mendapat perhatian dalam kempen ini.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: I also heard she didn't do ad spend on
paragraphs.cn: Saya juga dengar dia tidak membelanjakan wang untuk iklan -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Like only digital. Is that wise?
paragraphs.cn: Contohnya hanya iklan digital. Adakah itu bijak?

paragraphs.speaker: Rose
paragraphs.text: Well, I -- first of all, Donald Trump still has a stranglehold on the Republican primary. And that is why his party is getting ripped apart.
paragraphs.cn: Hmm, saya -- -- pertama, Donald Trump masih mengawal ketat pemilihan utama Republikan. Itulah sebabnya partinya sedang berpecah.

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.text: And didn't endorse her.
paragraphs.cn: Dan tidak menyokongnya.

paragraphs.speaker: Rose
paragraphs.text: Because the primary base is so extreme and they are so disassociated at this point from independents and moderates nationwide. But Nancy Mace took it one step further. She decided to antagonize, not just Donald Trump, but literally everyone in her life to include just random folks at the airport. So, what you saw here was -- and politicians do this sometimes. They jump into races not to win, but to bolster their public profile for something they're after.
paragraphs.cn: Kerana pengundi asas pemilihan utama sangat ekstrem, mereka kini sangat terputus hubungan dengan golongan bebas dan sederhana di peringkat nasional. Tetapi Nancy Mace melangkah lebih jauh. Dia memutuskan untuk menentang, bukan sahaja Donald Trump, tetapi hampir semua orang dalam hidupnya, termasuk orang rawak di lapangan terbang. Jadi, apa yang anda lihat di sini adalah -- -- ahli politik kadang-kadang melakukan ini. Mereka bertanding bukan untuk menang, tetapi untuk meningkatkan imej awam mereka demi mengejar matlamat tertentu.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Yes. Well, this is the kind of thing that is in my group chat because every member of the YOLO caucus, the people who limp back to Congress from their primaries, they're really upset. And they plan to spend the next couple of months antagonizing the White House. Do you think Nancy Mace will be one of those?
paragraphs.cn: Ya. Hmm, itulah yang ada dalam kumpulan sembang saya, kerana setiap ahli kaukus YOLO, mereka yang hampir-hampir tidak kembali ke Kongres dari pemilihan utama, sangat tidak puas hati. Mereka merancang untuk menentang Rumah Putih dalam beberapa bulan akan datang. Adakah anda fikir Nancy Mace akan menjadi salah seorang daripada mereka?

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.text: I think she's going to in her final months. I mean she's already burned all the bridges. She's -- I think the voters in her state didn't like the drama and they said, hey, we don't want that actually running the place.
paragraphs.cn: Saya fikir dia akan berbuat demikian dalam beberapa bulan terakhir. Maksud saya, dia sudah membakar semua jambatan. Dia -- -- saya rasa pengundi di negerinya tidak suka drama ini, mereka berkata, hei, kami tidak mahu orang seperti itu benar-benar mentadbir tempat ini.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Yes. That's a good point, you're not effective if you're at odds with everyone.
paragraphs.cn: Ya, betul. Jika anda tidak serasi dengan semua orang, anda tidak akan berkesan.

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.text: You're not effective. And you're infective (ph). And she's going to continue this. Obviously, if she's done it up to this point, I don't think she's going to stop. But I -- she's already burned the bridges with President Trump, clearly. So, what have you got to lose now?
paragraphs.cn: Anda tidak akan berkesan, dan anda juga akan menular (bunyi). Dia akan terus begini. Jelas sekali, jika dia sudah melakukannya sehingga sekarang, saya tidak fikir dia akan berhenti. Tetapi saya -- -- dia sudah putus hubungan sepenuhnya dengan Presiden Trump, jelas. Jadi, apa lagi yang anda boleh rugi sekarang?

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: What have you got to lose? OK, speaking of YOLO, we're going to talk about what's in our group chats on this news show. Mike Leon, what are you talking about in your group chats?
paragraphs.cn: Apa lagi yang anda boleh rugi? Baiklah, bercakap tentang YOLO, kami akan bincangkan apa yang ada dalam kumpulan sembang dalam rancangan berita ini. Mike Leon, apa yang kumpulan sembang anda bincangkan?

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.text: Well, first off, it's always the Knicks. But I'm talking about UFC 250. One thing that is always interesting to me --
paragraphs.cn: 嗯，首先，总是尼克斯队。但我在聊UFC 250。有一件事一直让我很感兴趣 --  --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: This is the UFC fight. The claws going up on the White House lawn.
paragraphs.cn: 这是UFC比赛。爪子伸到了白宫草坪上。

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.text: The UFC Freedom 250. Unnecessary. All of this that you're seeing here in the screen here. But Trump has always used the combat sports space to talk to voters. It's something I've talked to Democrats about. Senator Ruben Gallego, who's a big boxing fan, him and I have had this conversation. Democrat --
paragraphs.cn: UFC自由250。没必要。你在屏幕上看到的这一切。但特朗普一直利用格斗运动领域与选民对话。我和民主党人谈过这件事。参议员鲁本·加列戈是个大拳击迷，我和他有过这样的对话。民主党 --  --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: And they're giving tickets to combat (INAUDIBLE).
paragraphs.cn: 他们正在赠送格斗比赛的门票（听不清）。

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.text: Correct. And -- exactly. And Trump has always used it as a vehicle, for his relationship with Tyson and the fights that he would put on in Trump -- at Taj Mahal or whatever.
paragraphs.cn: 没错。而且 --  -- 正是如此。特朗普一直把它当作一个工具，用于他与泰森的关系以及他在特朗普泰姬陵或其他地方举办的比赛。

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.text: He would always be in this space. He's always had these relationships because those are the kind of voters that go out and vote for him. That's the thing that we've always talked about.
paragraphs.cn: 他总是在这个领域。他一直有这些关系，因为那些是出去投票给他的选民。这是我们一直谈论的事情。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: All right, my friend at "The Daily Signal," what's in your group chat?
paragraphs.cn: 好了，我在《每日信号》的朋友，你的群聊里有什么？

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.text: So, I'm going to bring up the Knicks because it's a big thing. I moved here to New York --
paragraphs.cn: 那么，我要提一下尼克斯队，因为这是件大事。我搬到纽约来 --  --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Why do I even bother?
paragraphs.cn: 我干嘛还要费这个劲？

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.text: I know. Why did you even bother.
paragraphs.cn: 我知道。你干嘛还要费这个劲。

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.text: Just change the segment.
paragraphs.cn: 换个话题吧。

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.text: You know, it matters to me. I'm from Oakland, California. The saddest sports town in America. We've lost all of our sports teams. So, just the fact the Knicks -- look, I have a lot of New York sports fan friends who haven't seen the Knicks or the Jets, or these teams ever do anything pretty much in their life, and now they get to spend some time with their kids and enjoy this moment. It may not come again. So, you know what --
paragraphs.cn: 你知道吗，这对我很重要。我来自加州奥克兰，美国最悲伤的体育城市。我们失去了所有的运动队。所以，就凭尼克斯队 --  -- 你看，我有很多纽约体育迷朋友，他们这辈子几乎没见过尼克斯队、喷气机队或其他队伍有什么作为，而现在他们能和孩子一起享受这一刻。这机会可能不会再有了。所以，你知道吗 --  --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: I love it. I love it. It's communal.
paragraphs.cn: Saya suka ini. Saya suka ini. Ini adalah pengalaman bersama.

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.text: It's communal. Its wonderful.
paragraphs.cn: Ini adalah pengalaman bersama. Hebat.

paragraphs.speaker: Rose
paragraphs.text: You know it, not just the Knicks, Donald Trump lost that game for the Knicks.
paragraphs.cn: Tahukah anda, bukan hanya pasukan Knicks, Donald Trump yang menyebabkan Knicks kalah dalam perlawanan itu.

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.text: Zero-11, 0-11 in sporting events.
paragraphs.cn: 0 kemenangan, 11 kekalahan dalam acara sukan.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: You're one of those people.
paragraphs.cn: Anda memang jenis orang itu.

paragraphs.speaker: Rose
paragraphs.text: Stay away. He lost it for us. We -- all we were doing is winning. This guy shows up.
paragraphs.cn: Jauhi. Dia menyebabkan kita kalah. Kita -- kita -- kita sentiasa menang. Lelaki ini muncul.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Well, luckily, he's not going. So, all of New York is not calling him the cooler.
paragraphs.cn: Nasib baik, dia tidak pergi. Jadi, seluruh New York tidak memanggilnya pembawa sial.

paragraphs.speaker: Rose
paragraphs.text: Fine. I thought I couldn't dislike the guy more.
